truth.papers.A32.title: The influence of silicon and aluminum alloying on the lattice parameter and stacking fault energy of austenitic steel
truth.papers.A32.paper_scope: Investigates the separate effects of Si and Al alloying on the austenite lattice parameter and stacking fault energy (SFE) in Fe-Ni-Cr-based austenitic steels.

prediction.papers.A32.title: The influence of silicon and aluminum alloying on the lattice parameter and stacking fault energy of austenitic steel
prediction.papers.A32.paper_scope: Experimental investigation of the effects of 2.5 wt.% Si and 2.5 wt.% Al alloying on the austenite lattice parameter and stacking fault energy in austenitic steels using XRD and weak-beam dark-field TEM.

truth.alloys.alloy_base.alloys_notes: Approximately the same base composition used for the three alloys, with variations in Si and Al contents.

Measure effect of Al alloying on austenite stacking fault energy

2.5 wt.% Al alloying increased SFE by 12.0 mJ/m¬≤ to 38.3 ¬Ī 6.1 mJ/m¬≤ (uncorrected). Upper-limit strain-field correction yields 44.0 ¬Ī 9.8 mJ/m¬≤. Linear SFE coefficient A_Al^SFE = 4.9 mJ m‚ĀĽ¬≤ wt.%‚ĀĽ¬Ļ.

truth.computational_details.idx_0.analytical_m: SFE calculation from partial dislocation separation
Calculate stacking fault energy (SFE) from measured partial dislocation separation d and character angle őĪ using isotropic elasticity theory.
SFE = [G |a0|^2 (2 - v) / (48ŌÄ (1 - v))] * (1/d) * (1 - 2v cos(2őĪ) / (2 - v)), with isotropic shear modulus G = 74 GPa, Poisson ratio v = 0.3, and experimental lattice parameter a0.
Elastic anisotropy effects explored using effective G = 66 GPa, v = 0.43 for 12Ni-12Cr steel, increased 2.5 Al SFE by ~10%, minimal effect on other alloys; isotropic values used for final results.

prediction.computational_details.idx_0.validation_a: XRD lattice parameters compared with linear empirical correlations from literature; TEM SFE values compared with literature data for similar Fe-Ni-Cr alloys.
prediction.computational_details.idx_0.analytical_m: Calculate stacking fault energy from measured partial dislocation separation and perfect dislocation character angle
Isotropic shear modulus G = 74 GPa, Poisson ratio őĹ = 0.3, lattice parameter a0 from XRD, partial dislocation separation d and character angle őĪ from WBDF TEM
SFE = G*|a0|¬≤*(2-őĹ)/(48*ŌÄ*(1-őĹ)) * (1/d) * (1 - 2*őĹ*cos(2őĪ)/(2-őĹ)). Elastic anisotropy correction evaluated (G=66 GPa, őĹ=0.43) but isotropic values used for primary results.
Empirical correlation between austenite composition and lattice parameter
A_Si^LP = -0.018 √ó 10‚ĀĽ¬≥ nm wt.%‚ĀĽ¬Ļ, A_Al^LP = 0.582 √ó 10‚ĀĽ¬≥ nm wt.%‚ĀĽ¬Ļ
Form: a0 = ő£(A_i^LP * x_i), where x_i is wt.% of element i. Values agree with Dyson and Holmes.
Empirical correlation between austenite composition and stacking fault energy
A_Si^SFE = -2.6 mJ m‚ĀĽ¬≤ wt.%‚ĀĽ¬Ļ, A_Al^SFE = 4.9 mJ m‚ĀĽ¬≤ wt.%‚ĀĽ¬Ļ
Form: SFE = ő£(A_i^SFE * x_i), where x_i is wt.% of element i.

truth.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: 2.5 wt.% Al alloying increased the austenite lattice parameter by 1.41 √ó 10^-3 nm relative to the base alloy, while 2.5 wt.% Si had a minimal effect. Linear lattice parameter coefficients A^LP found: Si = -0.018 √ó 10^-3 nm wt.%^-1, Al = 0.582 √ó 10^-3 nm wt.%^-1, consistent with Dyson and Holmes [16].
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_1.value: SFE results: Base alloy 26.3 ¬Ī 3.0 mJ m^-2; 2.5 Si alloy 19.9 ¬Ī 2.0 mJ m^-2; 2.5 Al alloy 38.3 ¬Ī 6.1 mJ m^-2. Linear SFE coefficients A^SFE found: Si = -2.6 mJ m^-2 wt.%^-1, Al = 4.9 mJ m^-2 wt.%^-1, in good agreement with thermochemical modeling for Al (~5.2 mJ m^-2 wt.%^-1) and high-Mn literature for Si (-1 to -3.5 mJ m^-2 wt.%^-1).
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_2.value: Ignoring Al effect on lattice parameter overestimates retained austenite C content by őī_C = x_Al (A_Al^LP / A_C^LP); for 1 wt.% Al, őī_C is estimated at 0.125‚Äď0.176 wt.%, which is non-negligible relative to the carbon levels that influence austenite thermodynamic stability.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: Williams and Carter [34] correction for partial dislocation strain fields was estimated but not applied to primary SFE data due to incomplete TEM parameters. Upper limit estimation suggests corrected SFE for 2.5 Al alloy would be 44.0 ¬Ī 9.8 mJ m^-2, while other alloys change by ~1 mJ m^-2 or less.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_4.value: Figure 1 shows a WBDF TEM micrograph of a dissociated dislocation in the base alloy, with annotations of d (partial separation) and őĪ (character angle), and a scale bar of 50 nm, illustrating the g//b imaging condition.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_5.value: Figure 2 scatter plot and Table 2 caption data show the dependence of austenite lattice parameter on relative Si and Al alloying (wt pct).
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_6.value: Figure 3(a) scatter plot presents measured partial dislocation separation vs. perfect dislocation character angle for all three alloys, with solid lines indicating average SFE and dashed lines showing standard deviation bounds. Figure 3(b) scatter plot shows dependence of SFE on relative Si and Al alloying (wt pct).
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_7.value: The paper's findings are limited to fully austenitic Fe-Ni-Cr steels; extension to multiphase low-alloy AHSS assumes Si and Al effects are independent of Ni and Cr alloying, and that no element partitioning occurs in the single-phase structure.

prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: XRD analysis showed the base alloy austenite lattice parameter is 0.3585 nm. 2.5 wt.% Al alloying increased the austenite lattice parameter by 1.41√ó10‚ĀĽ¬≥ nm to 0.3598 nm, whereas 2.5 wt.% Si alloying had little effect (0.3587 nm).
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_1.value: Ignoring Al alloying in austenite carbon content calculations based on lattice parameter may overestimate C content by 0.125 to 0.176 wt.% for 1 wt.% Al alloying, depending on the literature A_C^LP value used (3.30 to 4.67√ó10‚ĀĽ¬≥ nm wt.%‚ĀĽ¬Ļ).
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_2.value: Uncorrected weak-beam dark-field TEM measurements yielded stacking fault energies of 26.3 ¬Ī 3.0 mJ/m¬≤ for the base alloy, 38.3 ¬Ī 6.1 mJ/m¬≤ for the 2.5 Al alloy, and 19.9 ¬Ī 2.0 mJ/m¬≤ for the 2.5 Si alloy. Relative to the base alloy, 2.5 wt.% Al increased SFE by 12.0 mJ/m¬≤ and 2.5 wt.% Si decreased it by 6.4 mJ/m¬≤.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: The abstract and conclusion report SFE changes of 12‚Äď17 mJ/m¬≤ for 2.5 wt.% Al and 6‚Äď7 mJ/m¬≤ for 2.5 wt.% Si, likely reflecting the range including estimated upper-limit strain-field corrections (e.g., 44.0 ¬Ī 9.8 mJ/m¬≤ for the 2.5 Al alloy).
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_4.value: Figure 2 and Table 2 present the calculated austenite lattice parameter and empirical linear elemental coefficients for Si and Al (A^LP and A^SFE).
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_5.value: Figure 3(a) shows partial dislocation separation as a function of dislocation angle, and Figure 3(b) shows the dependence of SFE on relative Si and Al alloying, with solid lines representing average calculated SFE and dashed lines indicating upper and lower bounds from standard deviations.
